1: Naomi's initiative — 'Should I not seek a mānôaḥ (resting place/security) for you, that it may be well with you?' — echoes her earlier blessing over Orpah and Ruth at departure (Ruth 1:9: 'May YHWH grant that you find mānôaḥ'). The echo is deliberate: Naomi is now attempting to be the instrument of the mānôaḥ she had prayed YHWH would provide. The mother-in-law's obligation in an ANE household was precisely this: to use her social knowledge and connections to arrange advantageous matches for daughters-in-law. Naomi's scheme is not immoral but culturally conventional — she is fulfilling her household obligations through the mechanisms available to her.
2: The threshing floor (gōren H1637) was typically located on an elevated, exposed hillside where the prevailing westerly wind could separate grain from chaff. They were communal facilities — several families might share a threshing floor, or a large landowner like Boaz might maintain his own. Threshing occurred after harvest (typically May-June for wheat); the winnowing process of tossing grain in the air to let the chaff blow away required the late-afternoon breeze. Boaz's 'winnowing barley on the threshing floor tonight' sets the scene: he will eat (the harvest feast) and sleep on the threshing floor to protect the grain — a practice attested in ANE agriculture to prevent theft from the stored grain pile.
3: Naomi's instructions — 'wash yourself, put on fragrant oil, dress yourself in your best garments' — are preparations for a significant social encounter. Fragrant oil (šemen H8081) was used on festive occasions; its absence was a sign of mourning (2 Samuel 14:2; Daniel 10:3). Ruth's adoption of festive dress after the period of widowhood signals a transition in her social status — she is no longer performing mourning. The instruction 'do not make yourself known to the man until he has finished eating and drinking' reflects the social wisdom that a well-fed, satisfied man is more receptive to a significant request than a hungry one. The practical social psychology embedded in Naomi's instructions reflects real knowledge of how to navigate an ANE social encounter.
4: The instruction to uncover his feet (margelôtāyw, H4772 — a term used specifically for the area at someone's feet while sleeping) and lie down is debated by commentators. Some read margelôt as a euphemism; others understand it literally as exposing his feet to the night air to wake him. The most natural reading in context is that Ruth uncovers his feet to signal her presence at his feet in a position of petition and request. The foot/lower extremity as the position of a supplicant is culturally attested (the woman who anointed Jesus's feet, Luke 7:38; Mary sitting at Jesus's feet, Luke 10:39). Ruth positions herself as a petitioner seeking a legal act from Boaz, not merely a social visit.
5: 'All that you tell me I will do' — Ruth's complete compliance with Naomi's plan mirrors her earlier covenant declaration: 'where you go I will go' (Ruth 1:16-17). Ruth's obedience throughout the book is total and immediate; she does not debate, qualify, or second-guess the instructions she is given. This quality of obedient trust — in Naomi in Ruth 1-3, and implicitly in YHWH behind Naomi's instructions — is the behavioral content of hesed. The narrative presents Ruth's obedience not as passivity but as active covenant loyalty expressed through the medium of following trustworthy guidance.
6: The threshing floor scene at night creates dramatic intensity. Boaz's eating, drinking, and lying down at the end of the grain heap is a naturalistic detail of harvest celebration: the threshing floor feast (a form of first-fruits celebration) was a moment of communal gratitude and abundance, typically involving communal eating and the sleeping of the workers on-site to guard the grain overnight. The scene is set at night, in the open air, at a time of seasonal celebration — a context that makes the encounter intimate and unusual without being necessarily scandalous.
7: Boaz 'was in good spirits' (wayyîṭaḇ libbô) — a phrase used elsewhere for the pleasant mood following a feast (Judges 16:25 of Samson; 1 Samuel 25:36 of Nabal). The combination of harvest completion, communal celebration, and satisfied rest creates the particular moment Naomi has calculated for Ruth's approach. The detail that Boaz lay down 'at the end of the heap of grain' is significant: he is positioned to protect the stored grain, a task important enough that he sleeps beside it. Ruth's approach to him there is not an intrusion on a private sleeping chamber but a visible public space in the context of legitimate harvest guardianship.
8: 'At midnight the man was startled and turned over' — the dramatic midnight revelation. The hour of midnight in OT narrative is associated with decisive divine action: the exodus death of the firstborn (Exodus 12:29), Samuel's night call (1 Samuel 3:3ff), and here YHWH's providential arrangement reaching its human climax at midnight. Boaz's discovery of a woman at his feet in the dark is arresting — he does not immediately know who she is (v9: 'Who are you?'), which means the encounter is genuinely surprising to him, not a pre-arranged liaison.
9: Ruth's request — 'Spread your wing (kānāp̄) over your servant, for you are a redeemer (go'el)' — is the book's legal-theological climax. Kānāp̄ (H3671, wing/corner of a garment) is the same word Boaz used in his blessing in Ruth 2:12 ('under whose wings you have come to take refuge') — Ruth is asking Boaz to be the answer to his own prayer. The spreading of one's kānāp̄ over a woman was a legal act of claiming her as a wife (Ezekiel 16:8: YHWH spreads his kānāp̄ over Israel; Deuteronomy 23:1; Ruth 27:20). Ruth's request is simultaneously a marriage proposal and a legal go'el petition: she is asking Boaz to exercise his kinsman-redeemer right to marry her and perpetuate the dead's name.
10: Boaz's response — 'May you be blessed by YHWH, my daughter. You have made this last kindness greater than the first' — explicitly calls Ruth's action hesed and identifies two acts of hesed: the first (leaving Moab and caring for Naomi) and the second (this approach to Boaz rather than pursuing younger men). The 'young men, whether poor or rich' reflects the social reality that Ruth had options; her choice of the go'el route rather than a young husband reflects hesed toward the dead (perpetuating the family name) over personal advantage. Boaz's praise encodes the book's theological insight: hesed is love that costs something and benefits those who may never repay it.
11: 'All my townspeople know that you are a woman of valor (ʾēšet ḥayil)' — the same ḥayil used for Boaz in Ruth 2:1. The public reputation Ruth has built over two months of harvest gleaning — her diligence, her care for Naomi, her integrity — is now the foundation for Boaz's assurance. Proverbs 31:10 opens with ʾēšet ḥayil mî yimṣāʾ ('a woman of valor, who can find?'), the same rare idiom; the Proverbs 31 portrait is often read as the idealized Israelite woman, and Ruth is its narrative embodiment. The fact that 'all the gate of my people knows' indicates that Ruth's reputation has penetrated the town's official legal-social space — the gate where elders deliberate (Ruth 4).
12: Boaz's disclosure — 'there is a redeemer nearer than I' — introduces the legal complication that will require resolution in Ruth 4. The go'el institution operated by proximity of kinship: the nearest male kinsman had first right and obligation. Boaz's acknowledgment that a closer kinsman exists shows his legal integrity — he will not circumvent the other man's prior right, even though he desires to act. The existence of a nearer go'el is a narrative problem that Ruth 4 must solve, and it also demonstrates that YHWH's providential arrangement requires proper legal process rather than shortcuts.
13: Boaz's commitment — 'As YHWH lives, I will redeem you' — invokes the divine name in a binding oath (the same formula as 1 Samuel 14:45; Ruth 20:21; 2 Samuel 15:21). The condition 'if he will redeem you, good; let him redeem. But if he is not willing to redeem you, then, as YHWH lives, I will redeem you' is legally precise: Boaz will give the nearer go'el his legal opportunity first, and if he refuses, Boaz will act. The commitment is unconditional within those legal parameters. 'Lie down until morning' — the instruction to remain for the night rather than making a furtive departure in the dark is an act of protective hospitality: leaving before dawn in the dark could expose Ruth to danger on the road.
14: Ruth's pre-dawn departure is timed to preserve social propriety: 'She rose before one could recognize another' (the phrase indicates it was dark enough to prevent identification). Boaz's instruction 'Let it not be known that the woman came to the threshing floor' is social protection for Ruth's reputation — the encounter, while legally and morally legitimate, could be misread if publicly known before the gate proceedings of Ruth 4. The six measures of barley Boaz gives her serve as both practical provision and a sign to Naomi (v15-17: Naomi asks 'how did you fare?' and the grain tells the story before Ruth speaks).
15: Six measures of barley (the unit is likely seʾāh, about 8 liters each = ~48 liters total, roughly 37 kg) is a substantial load — almost too heavy for one person to carry comfortably. The quantity signals that Boaz is sending a message to Naomi as much as providing for Ruth. 'He went into the city' — the note that Boaz immediately returned to the town (not staying at the threshing floor) suggests he has a plan he is ready to execute. The threshing floor encounter has moved both characters to action: Ruth returns to Naomi, Boaz moves to the gate.
16: Naomi's question — 'How did you fare, my daughter?' (literally: 'Who are you, my daughter?' — an idiom asking about her status/condition) — gets the answer through Ruth's telling of the story and through the six measures of barley as physical evidence. The grain speaks: Boaz has sent a practical signal that the matter is not finished and that provision continues. Naomi's reading is exact (v17: 'He gave me these six measures of barley, saying “Do not go back empty-handed to your mother-in-law”') — she correctly interprets the grain as a message.
17: 'Do not go back empty-handed to your mother-in-law' — Boaz's instruction to Ruth echoes Naomi's own complaint in Ruth 1:21 ('I went out full and YHWH brought me back empty'). Boaz's provision is YHWH's answer to Naomi's emptiness: the grain is the beginning of the reversal. The word 'empty' (rêqāh) used in 1:21 and here connects the two scenes deliberately — the narrator wants the reader to see the provision as the start of YHWH's answer to Naomi's lament. YHWH does not answer the lament with an explanation but with grain, and through grain with a redeemer.
18: Naomi's instruction — 'Sit still, my daughter, until you learn how the matter will fall, for the man will not rest but will settle the matter today' — is a word of faith: she trusts that Boaz will act quickly. 'Will not rest' (yišqōṭ, the same root as the book's key word mānôaḥ — rest/settle) until he settles the matter uses the rest vocabulary in reverse: Boaz will not be at rest until he has provided rest for Ruth. The mother-in-law's confident reading of Boaz's character — that the six measures of barley and the sworn commitment mean immediate action — reflects her knowledge both of Boaz and of what YHWH is doing through him.
